questions.question: Which statement best captures what **scalability** means?
options: The ability to handle growing load by adding resources, with cost rising roughly in proportion to capacity
The raw speed at which a single request is served end to end
The guarantee that every read returns the most recent write
The percentage of time a system is available to serve requests
questions.explanation: Scalability is about handling growth by adding resources with cost rising no faster than capacity. Raw single-request speed is performance, not scalability — a fast single box can be unscalable; consistency and availability are separate properties.

questions.question: Which is a genuine drawback of **vertical** scaling (scale up) that **horizontal** scaling avoids?
options: The node remains a single point of failure with a hard hardware ceiling
It forces every service to be rewritten as stateless
It introduces replication lag between the nodes
It requires a load balancer in front of the node
questions.explanation: Scaling up keeps one box (a SPOF) and hits a hard ceiling (the biggest machine you can buy), with cost growing super-linearly at the top. Statelessness, replication lag, and load balancers are concerns of the horizontal/distributed approach, not penalties of scaling up.

questions.question: Calling a service "stateless" means the overall system holds no state anywhere.
questions.explanation: Stateless refers to the *service* keeping no client-specific state in process memory between requests. State doesn't vanish — it is externalized to a database, distributed cache, object storage, or a self-contained token. The system still has state.

questions.question: A **Layer 4** load balancer routes traffic based on what?
options: IP address and port, without reading the payload
HTTP path, host, header, and cookie values
The TLS certificate's subject name after termination
The geographic location of the client
questions.explanation: L4 operates at the transport layer on IP + port and does not read the payload, making it fast and protocol-agnostic. Routing by HTTP path/host/header/cookie and TLS termination are L7 capabilities.

questions.question: Why should you report **p95/p99** latency rather than the average?
options: Averages hide the slow tail — a few very slow requests barely move the mean but are what users notice
Averages are mathematically impossible to compute on streaming data
Percentiles always produce a smaller number than the mean, which looks better
The average over-weights the slow requests and exaggerates the problem
questions.explanation: One 10s request among ninety-nine 10ms requests barely moves the mean but wrecks p99. The tail, not the average, is what users complain about — especially under high fan-out where the slowest call dominates the page.

questions.question: **Elasticity** (autoscaling) requires statelessness. Why can't you safely autoscale nodes that hold in-memory user sessions?
options: Removing a node on scale-down would destroy the only copy of those users' session state
Stateful nodes cannot be placed behind a load balancer at all
Autoscalers can only add nodes, never remove them, so state would accumulate
In-memory sessions make the node respond too slowly to be added to a pool
questions.explanation: Elasticity adds nodes on a spike and removes them when idle; you can't safely kill a node that holds the only copy of a user's session. Externalizing session state lets any node serve any request, so scale-down is safe.

questions.question: Why are **sticky sessions (session affinity)** considered a smell? Select all that apply.
options: They defeat even load distribution across the backend pool
They break when the pinned node dies, dropping that client's state
They complicate autoscaling
They are required for any HTTPS traffic to work
They make the database the bottleneck by routing all writes to one shard
questions.explanation: Stickiness is a crutch for stateful servers: it skews load distribution, loses state when the node dies, and complicates autoscaling. It is unrelated to HTTPS, and it does not route DB writes to a single shard — externalizing the session removes the need for it entirely.

questions.question: A service receives **800 RPS** with an average response time of **250 ms**. Using Little's Law, roughly how many requests are in flight on average, and how many nodes are needed at minimum if one node handles ~50 concurrent?
options: ~200 concurrent → at least 4 nodes (plus headroom)
~50 concurrent → at least 1 node (plus headroom)
~3,200 concurrent → at least 64 nodes (plus headroom)
~16 concurrent → at least 1 node (plus headroom)
questions.explanation: L = λ × W = 800 × 0.25 = 200 concurrent. 200 / 50 = 4 nodes at the limit, so you run 4 plus headroom and never at 100% utilization.

questions.question: Running a server at ~100% CPU utilization is the most efficient operating point because no capacity is wasted.
questions.explanation: As utilization ρ approaches 1, queue length and wait time grow roughly proportional to 1/(1−ρ), so latency blows up. Target ~70–80% to leave headroom for bursts — 100% is a latency disaster, not efficiency.

questions.question: A read-heavy API's database CPU pegs at peak while the stateless app tier sits idle. Per the escalation ladder, what is the **cheapest** effective first move?
options: Cache the hot reads (in-memory / Redis) to remove load before it hits the DB
Shard the database across multiple nodes by a chosen key
Add more stateless app nodes behind the load balancer
Add read replicas and route all reads to them
questions.explanation: The ladder goes cheap→expensive: cache reads first, then optimize queries/indexes, then scale up, then replicas, then shard. Adding app nodes in front of an overloaded DB only makes it fall over faster; replicas and sharding come later and cost more.

questions.question: Your single primary database is saturating on **write** throughput (not reads). Which technique actually addresses this?
options: Sharding (horizontal partitioning) — split rows across nodes by a key
Adding more read replicas to offload the primary
Putting a read-through cache in front of the database
Switching the load balancer from L4 to L7
questions.explanation: Read replicas and caches scale reads; every write still hits the single primary. Sharding splits rows across nodes and is the technique that scales writes and storage. A load balancer change is irrelevant to DB write capacity.

questions.question: With asynchronously-updated read replicas, a user updates their profile and immediately reloads the page but sees the **old** values. What is happening and a valid mitigation?
options: Replication lag served a stale replica; route that user's reads to the primary briefly after a write
The cache TTL expired; lower the cache TTL to zero for all users
The shard key is hot; pick a more evenly distributed shard key
The load balancer lost affinity; enable sticky sessions for all clients
questions.explanation: This is the read-your-own-writes problem: async replicas lag, so a read just after a write may hit a not-yet-updated replica. Mitigate by reading the writer for that user briefly, or using synchronous/quorum reads where correctness demands it.

questions.question: A system serves **100 million reads/day**. Using the back-of-the-envelope recipe (86,400 s/day, ~5× peak factor), the **peak** RPS is closest to:
options: ~290 RPS
questions.explanation: Average RPS = 100,000,000 / 86,400 ≈ 1,160. Peak ≈ 1,160 × 5 ≈ 5,800 RPS. 1,160 is the average (not peak); 290 is the resulting concurrency at 50 ms response time, not RPS.

questions.question: In this ASP.NET Core configuration, what is the scalability purpose of registering the distributed Redis cache before `AddSession()`?
options: Session state lives in Redis, not node memory, so any node can serve any request
It encrypts session cookies so they cannot be tampered with by the client
It pins each user to one node via a consistent hash for faster lookups
It enables L7 path-based routing at the load balancer
questions.explanation: Backing sessions with Redis externalizes the state, so the service stays stateless and any node can read the session — enabling load balancing, autoscaling, and rolling restarts. It is not about cookie encryption, node pinning, or LB routing.

questions.question: Why is "a queue without back-pressure a **delayed crash**, not a fix"?
options: An unbounded queue merely buffers in front of the bottleneck; the buffer keeps growing until memory runs out
Queues add network hops that always increase end-to-end latency beyond acceptable limits
At-least-once delivery guarantees duplicates, which corrupt the datastore
Workers cannot be scaled independently of producers when a queue is involved
questions.explanation: A queue doesn't remove the bottleneck — it buffers ahead of it. If arrival rate exceeds drain rate and the queue is unbounded, it grows without limit until memory is exhausted. Back-pressure (bounded queues, shedding, rate limits) is what prevents the delayed crash.

questions.question: You move slow PDF generation behind a queue with workers. Why must the worker be **idempotent**?
options: At-least-once delivery means a message may be processed more than once, so duplicate processing must be safe
Idempotency is what makes the API return `202 Accepted` instead of `200 OK`
It guarantees the result is ready synchronously when the original request returns
It prevents replication lag between the queue and the database
questions.explanation: Async queues are designed for at-least-once delivery, so duplicates will happen; idempotent consumers make reprocessing the same message harmless. It has nothing to do with the 202 status, synchronous results, or replication lag.

questions.question: Which statements about **shard key** choice and sharding trade-offs are correct? Select all that apply.
options: A poorly chosen key creates hot shards (skew) and forces expensive cross-shard fan-out queries
Hash sharding spreads data evenly but kills efficient range scans
Consistent hashing minimizes data movement when shards are added or removed
Sharding makes cross-shard transactions and joins trivial, which is its main appeal
Range sharding always distributes load perfectly evenly regardless of key
questions.explanation: Bad keys cause hot shards and fan-out; hash sharding spreads evenly but breaks range scans; consistent hashing minimizes reshuffling. Cross-shard transactions/joins are hard (often avoided or denormalized), and range sharding risks hotspots on sequential keys rather than guaranteeing even load.

questions.question: When configuring CDN caching, there is a subtle correctness hazard around **personalized** responses. What goes wrong if it is mishandled?
options: A bad `Vary` / cache key can serve one user's personalized response to a different user
Fingerprinted filenames like `app.9f3c.js` cause the browser to re-download on every request
Setting `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` forces revalidation on every hit
`stale-while-revalidate` blocks the response until the origin confirms freshness
questions.explanation: Personalized content must be marked `private`/`no-store` or vary correctly; a bad `Vary` or cache key can leak one user's data to another. Fingerprinted names enable long caching (new deploy = new URL), `immutable` avoids revalidation, and `stale-while-revalidate` serves fast and refreshes in the background.
